items.mistake: Treating the mock as a quiz — solve in silence, hope the interviewer figures out your thinking.
items.fix: Narrate every step: 'I'm going to try a hash map first because…'.
items.why: Interviewers grade your *thinking* as much as your code; silence is unscorable.
items.example:
```
Candidate: 'Two options here — set for O(1) membership or sort for two-pointer pair search. Set is simpler for this size, let me start there.'
```

items.fix: Spend at least 10 minutes asking what stood out, what was unclear, what the interviewer would change.
items.why: The mock is the diagnostic; the debrief is where you learn what to drill next.
items.example: Top three debrief questions: 'What was the weakest moment?', 'Where did I lose you?', 'If you were hiring me, would you?'

items.mistake: Stacking too many mocks without targeted practice.
items.fix: Every mock surfaces a top weakness; spend the next 2-3 days drilling just that.
items.why: Mocks reveal gaps; closing them requires focused, slow practice — not another mock.
items.example: Mock revealed shaky DP recurrences → drill 1 DP problem/day for a week before the next mock.

items.fix: Rotate partners across skill levels and backgrounds.
items.why: A single partner's blind spots become your blind spots; different perspectives reveal different gaps.
items.example: Alternate peer mocks (cheap, high-volume) with senior mocks (rare, high-signal).

items.mistake: Skipping system-design and behavioural mocks because coding feels harder.
items.fix: Schedule at least one of each in any 4-week prep cycle.
items.why: Most loops include all three; weak behavioural answers sink otherwise strong candidates.
items.example: Behavioural mock: practice 5 STAR stories covering disagreement, failure, leadership, ambiguity, and impact.
